tasks.task_name: Find warm backpacking destination (1-2 night, mid-March)
tasks.first_domino: Open Google and search 'warm backpacking March weekend US'
tasks.hook_line: Where can you backpack in March that's warm enough to enjoy but not booked solid?
tasks.reward: Locks in the destination so you can book flights and focus on gear
nuggets: Red Rock Canyon NV permits not required
Joshua Tree CA campgrounds reservable now
tasks.system_prompt: Help me find a warm backpacking destination for a 1-2 night trip on the weekend of March 14-15, 2026. I'm in the US and need somewhere with daytime temps above 65°F. Looking for a moderate trail with good scenery — this is for my friend's 30th birthday celebration. After this we're flying to Chicago for the actual party. I want 3-4 solid options with trail names, permit requirements, nearby airports, and current weather forecasts. Prioritize places with easy logistics and availability.

tasks.task_name: Figure out REI gear rentals (backpack, tent, sleeping bag)
tasks.first_domino: Go to REI.com and search 'gear rental backpacking'
tasks.hook_line: REI rents full backpacking kits — first time trying rental instead of buying
tasks.reward: Answers the gear question without a $500+ purchase commitment
tasks.system_prompt: I need to rent backpacking gear from REI for a 1-2 night trip on March 14-15. I need a backpack (65L+), 2-person tent, and sleeping bag rated for ~40-50°F nights. Help me understand REI's rental process: what's available at my local store (search for stores near my zip code if needed), pricing, how far in advance to reserve, pickup/return logistics, and whether there are package deals. Also let me know if there are better alternatives to REI for short-term gear rental.

tasks.first_domino: Open Google Flights and enter destination airport
tasks.hook_line: 8 days out — flight prices will jump if you wait much longer
tasks.reward: Commits the trip logistics and unblocks planning the actual route
nuggets: Southwest often best for last-minute domestic
Allegiant serves some desert airports cheap
tasks.system_prompt: I need to book roundtrip flights for the weekend of March 14-15 to [DESTINATION — fill this in once I pick the backpacking location]. Flying from [MY HOME AIRPORT]. Need to depart Friday March 14 early/mid-day and return Sunday March 16 or fly direct to Chicago. Compare Southwest, United, and budget carriers. Show me the cheapest options that don't have brutal layovers. I'm flexible on times but want to maximize time on the trail.

tasks.task_name: Book flight from backpacking to Chicago
tasks.first_domino: Open Google Flights and search [backpacking airport] to Chicago
tasks.hook_line: Chicago flights are booked — this is the last flight piece
tasks.reward: Completes all travel logistics so you can focus on the fun
nuggets: Direct flights under 3hrs from most Southwest hubs
March 16 evening arrival works for party prep
tasks.system_prompt: I need a one-way flight from [BACKPACKING DESTINATION AIRPORT] to Chicago on Sunday March 16, 2026. I'm going to Mariano's 30th birthday party — the actual birthday is March 19 but I want to arrive Sunday evening to have time to settle in. I already have a flight back home from Chicago. Compare Midway and O'Hare. Show me options that land by 8pm Sunday so I'm not wrecked Monday. Budget matters but reliability matters more.

tasks.first_domino: Text Mariano's partner or check his Instagram for hints
tasks.hook_line: What do you get a 30-year-old who probably doesn't need anything?
tasks.reward: Shows up with something thoughtful instead of scrambling at O'Hare
nuggets: Experience gifts work well for milestone birthdays
tasks.system_prompt: Help me brainstorm a 30th birthday gift for my friend Mariano. We're doing a backpacking trip and then celebrating in Chicago. I don't want to carry anything heavy on the trail, so either a Chicago-delivery option or something small/experiential. Budget ~$50-100. He's into [INSERT WHAT YOU KNOW ABOUT MARIANO — outdoors? food? drinks? music?]. I want something thoughtful but not overly sentimental. Give me 5 ideas with where to buy and estimated delivery times.

tasks.task_name: Look up eyeglasses stores in Kyoto for early trip shopping
tasks.hook_line: Kyoto might have better frame selection than Tokyo — and you'll be fresh off the plane
tasks.reward: Gets glasses shopping done early so you're not scrambling in Tokyo at the end
nuggets: Zoff and JINS have Kyoto locations near station
Kyoto Eyevan known for artisan frames
English service common in central Kyoto shops
tasks.system_prompt: Help me find eyeglasses stores in Kyoto where I can buy 4 pairs of prescription glasses. I'm arriving March 25 and staying until March 27. I want affordable but stylish options — Japanese brands like Zoff, JINS, or Owndays are perfect. I need stores with English-speaking staff or easy self-service kiosks. Give me 3-4 store recommendations with addresses, Google Maps links, typical price ranges, and whether they can do same-day prescriptions. Also let me know what the process is like for foreigners buying glasses in Japan.

tasks.task_name: Validate insurance/prescription requirements for buying 4 pairs of glasses
tasks.first_domino: Call insurance provider and ask about Japan glasses reimbursement
tasks.hook_line: If insurance covers it, 4 pairs in Japan could save $400+ vs US prices
tasks.reward: Confirms you can actually expense these and aren't wasting money
nuggets: Most US vision insurance covers out-of-country
Need itemized receipt in English for reimbursement
Prescription must be <2 years old usually
tasks.system_prompt: I'm buying 4 pairs of prescription eyeglasses in Japan (March 25-April 7, 2026) and want to understand the logistics. Help me figure out: (1) Will my US vision insurance reimburse for foreign purchases? What documentation do I need? (2) Do I need to bring my physical prescription or will the stores measure me? (3) Are there any customs/import limits on bringing back 4 pairs of glasses? (4) What's the typical cost for frames + lenses at Japanese chains like Zoff or JINS? I want to make sure this is actually cheaper than buying in the US.

tasks.first_domino: Open Google Maps and search 'best temples Kyoto'
tasks.hook_line: Kyoto has 2000+ temples — which ones are actually worth the early wake-up?
tasks.reward: Closes out Kyoto planning — only Takayama and Tokyo left
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan activities for 2.5 days in Kyoto (March 25-27, 2026). Traveling with my girlfriend Dana. We like temples, walking, outdoor spaces, and good food. Already have hotels booked in central Kyoto. I want a day-by-day itinerary with opening hours, Google Maps links, and a suggested walking route. Include a mix of major landmarks (Fushimi Inari, Kinkaku-ji, Arashiyama) and less touristy spots. Also recommend 2-3 restaurants per day for lunch and dinner. We don't need to see everything — prioritize quality over quantity.

tasks.first_domino: Google 'things to do Takayama old town walking'
tasks.hook_line: Takayama is the mountain town break between temple-heavy Kyoto and Tokyo chaos
tasks.reward: Finishes the mid-trip section so the whole route is mapped
nuggets: Hida beef skewers famous street food
Shinhotaka Ropeway alpine views ~90min from town
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan 1.5 days in Takayama, Japan (late March 2026). This is a breather stop between Kyoto and Tokyo. We want a mix of old town wandering, nature/mountain views, and good food (especially Hida beef). Hotel is near Takayama Station. Give me a loose itinerary with morning market timing, walking routes through the old town, any worthwhile day trips (like Shirakawa-go or alpine areas), and restaurant recommendations. We're not in a rush here — this is the 'slow down' part of the trip.

tasks.first_domino: Open Google Maps and pin Tokyo neighborhoods (Shibuya, Shinjuku, Asakusa)
tasks.hook_line: Tokyo is massive — how do you balance weird neighborhoods, food, and shopping in 4 days?
tasks.reward: Completes all Japan planning — the entire trip is now mapped
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan 4 days in Tokyo (early April 2026). Traveling with Dana. We want a mix of food (ramen, sushi, izakayas), shopping (stationery, limited edition fountain pens, quirky stores), some culture (temples/museums but not too heavy), and nightlife (bars, karaoke). Hotels are in Shinjuku. Give me a neighborhood-by-neighborhood breakdown with must-dos, food recs, and a sense of pacing. We want to experience Tokyo's energy without overscheduling. Include day/night suggestions since Tokyo transforms after dark.

tasks.first_domino: Open VS Code and pull latest from task-pilot repo
tasks.hook_line: The extension shell works — now it's about making it actually useful
tasks.reward: Gets you closer to a working prototype you can dogfood in real projects
nuggets: Task state can live in .vscode folder
tasks.system_prompt: I'm building a VS Code extension called AI Task Pilot to help with AI-assisted development while maintaining focus. The extension should visualize work in progress and provide AI (like Cursor) with context about ongoing tasks. I want it to: (1) Track active tasks in a sidebar, (2) Let me update a shared working document locally, (3) Feed that context to AI conversations. I'm using TypeScript and the VS Code extension API. The project started 3 days ago — basic shell is set up. Help me prioritize the next features to build and sketch out the architecture for task state management and AI context injection.

tasks.first_domino: Sketch out sidebar layout on paper or Figma
tasks.hook_line: How do you show work-in-progress without creating more distraction than you're solving?
tasks.reward: Defines the UX so you're building toward a clear vision
nuggets: VS Code webview API supports React
Task cards need collapse/expand for focus
tasks.system_prompt: Help me design the UI for a VS Code extension that visualizes active tasks in a sidebar. The goal is helping ADHD developers stay focused by showing what they're working on and what's next. The sidebar should show: current task, task description/context, next 2-3 tasks in queue, and a way to quickly switch or mark done. It needs to be glanceable but not distracting. VS Code sidebars are narrow (~300px). Sketch out component structure, key interactions, and visual hierarchy. Reference examples from existing task/todo extensions if helpful.

tasks.first_domino: Research VS Code extension API for workspace file access
tasks.hook_line: Can you inject task context into AI conversations without copy-paste hell?
tasks.reward: Creates the core value prop — seamless AI context awareness
nuggets: Context injected via markdown snippets works
tasks.system_prompt: I'm building a VS Code extension that feeds task context to AI assistants like Cursor. The idea: I update a working document locally (markdown file in .vscode folder), and the extension makes that available to AI conversations without manual copy-paste. Help me design the context management system: (1) Where should task state live? (2) How do I make it readable by Cursor/Copilot? (3) What's the best format — markdown, JSON, custom DSL? (4) How do I keep it in sync as tasks change? I want it to feel automatic — the AI just 'knows' what I'm working on.

recommended_slots.reason: Urgent deadline (8 days), high-impact decision that unblocks all other trip logistics, and perfect for ramp_up research energy
Low activation barrier research task that feeds into glasses shopping strategy, pairs well with morning energy
Quick win (30 min), low context cost, and batches naturally with slot 1 — both outdoor gear research
